data.title: Sixth Street Specialty: Third Most Expensive BDC, And I'm Still Holding
data.description:
Earlier in mid-June, this year, I wrote an article on Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) titled: Sixth Street Specialty: Hold, Sell, Or Double Down? My BDC Battle.

As the title suggests, the case was not so straight forward for me. The reason lied in the premium that was almost at the historical high. However, given my assumption of a forthcoming BDC storm and the overall strategy of long-term dividend investing in high-quality names, I decided to maintain a bullish stance.

Since then, TSLX has registered around 6% in total returns, which is 2x above the result achieved by the overall BDC index (BIZD).

What this, however, means is that we have now an even higher priced TSLX.

The premium over NAV has jumped to 42%, making TSLX the third most expensive BDC out there. From all of the externally managed BDCs, TSLX is the most expensive one.

With this backdrop in mind, let's take a look at the freshly baked earnings report, which, in my view, carries some very important data points for us to digest.

Thesis Review

This time I would like to start with a quite important quote from Joshua William Easterly - CEO & Chairman of the Board - who in the most recent earnings call basically validated my bearish stance on the BDC sector:

I mean in this earnings script, if there is complacency, which is we think investors are looking at the historical return backward-looking LTM, which is higher than the forward, given both the combination of the higher spread in the back book compared to reinvestment spreads today plus the difference between the downward sloping SOFR curve. And so you have spot SOFR, which is somewhere between 80 and 90 basis points above the SOFR swap curve. And so as people do, they kind of look at things and say, what's the return profile been? But we think the return profile is going lower. And that is a -- that needs to shake out. I would expect that, that will shake out and the flows will change, flow get reallocated to those managers that have been able to continue to produce in the new environment and attractive ROE.

You can find more details about the BDC sector-wide headwinds in my article - here - but the main essence is that both interest rate and spread environment are no longer as accommodating for BDCs as a couple of quarters and years ago.

These are systematic pressures, which cannot be directly avoided.

The only way is to absorb them and make sure that there are strong enough offsets in place to combat the consequences from, say, unattractive spreads and downwards-sloping SOFR.

The question is whether TSLX has what it takes - i.e., to safeguard the existing dividend without sacrificing the NAV.

Looking at the Q2 2025 earnings report, in my view, the answer is still yes.

The adjusted NII per share came in at $0.56, which is well above the base distribution of $0.46 per share. So, we have a dividend that is covered by 127%.

Granted, the coverage level has been on a rather constant decline since early 2024, but there are three good news: 1) the rate of change is not that material, and 2) going forward we should see a more stabilized NII per share generation, and 3) as mentioned above, the dividend remains nicely covered.

The reason why I believe that the NII per share will likely remain stable is related to the fact that TSLX is no longer subject to the lagged effects of base rate cuts that were made back in late 2024. Even though TSLX has perfectly aligned floating debt base with floating asset base, it still was forced to register NII per share decline due to the part of asset base that is funded by non-variable rate (permanent) equity.

Of course, if the Fed decides to keep trimming down the rates, TSLX will once again have to deal with the consequences.

Yet, here we have to factor in the proceeds that TSLX retains each quarter in its books. Namely, this undistributed proceeds can (and are) redeployed back in the business to either de-lever or expand the asset base. Both of these items are inherently positive to the NII per share generation, which should allow TSLX to accumulate incremental earnings power that could then be used as an offset for future headwinds down the road.

To execute on this, it is critical that the portfolio quality remains in great shape. Otherwise, if TSLX will have to deal with additional issues in the form of non-accruals, then the picture could indeed get ugly. Not necessarily on the dividend coverage front because the margin of safety is so high, but more on the P/NAV side, which would give the market a justified base for drastically correcting TSLX multiples to the downside. And it is a long way to fall for TSLX (remember, it is the third most expensive BDC).

However, the data points we see are looking solid. For example, the current non-accrual rate is lower than in the previous quarter, landing at 0.6% on a fair value basis. You will struggle to find another BDC with this low non-accrual balance. Plus, TSLX continues to have conservative weighted average attachment and detachment points for their portfolio companies of 0.3x and 5.0x, respectively. Plus, as of Q2, 2025, the weighted average interest coverage remained at 2.1x, which is an indication of a very healthy portfolio.

The Bottom-Line

TSLX is expensive, no doubt about it.

The premium is very high given the unfavorable BDC outlook (also confirmed by the CEO of TSLX).

So, I would not suggest overweighting or loading a ton of capital into TSLX.

However, given the inherent dividend durability and portfolio quality, TSLX remains a solid investment case for yield-seeking investors. TSLX has the necessary level of margin of safety to weather forthcoming storms without experiencing permanent declines in NAV and/or dividend reductions.

This is why I have decided to keep the position in my portfolio (although, I am not adding or reinvesting more).

data.title: 8 Of My Favourite Dividend Stocks To Survive - And Thrive - In This Market
data.description:
Introduction

I think we can all agree on one thing: these are exciting times.

Last year, I wrote an article titled "Buckle Up Buttercup: My Warning To Dividend Investors." So far, I believe I haven't overpromised, as 2025 has been driven by daily trade headlines, soaring gold and crypto prices, political headlines, rapid AI advancements, fears over government debt stability, and much more.

While it may seem like I'm tooting my own horn, I believe I have written some of my most important articles in recent years this year. This includes an article titled "The Paradigm Shift is Coming - Here's How To Prepare Your Portfolio."

In that article, I highlighted the shifting plans in the nation's capital when it comes to managing debt, inflation, and economic growth, using one of my most trusted sources:

A growing consensus is emerging among the top ranks of U.S. economic policymakers: 4% real growth and 4% inflation may not just be acceptable - they might be the new target.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, installed earlier this year under President Donald Trump’s second administration, has reportedly outlined a framework targeting 8% to 9% nominal GDP growth, signaling a sharp departure from the fiscal restraint and inflation sensitivity of recent decades. According to officials familiar with his views, Bessent believes this level of growth is both achievable and desirable - even at the cost of bond market stability. - Albert Marko

Essentially, the government has signaled that it is willing to boost growth, even if it risks higher inflation. It's a super risky strategy, but if it works, it could result in much higher real growth and a relatively lower real debt load, as post-inflation debt/GDP could end up being much more favorable.

As the "external risks & opportunities" pillar of my investment strategy is based on geopolitics, I believe it's (by far) the most important pillar when it comes to finding the right strategies.

That's also why I am a lot more focused on cyclical value and inflation protection stocks, as inflation risks were already high going into this year.

Moreover, I continue to see confirmation of my thesis, as The Wall Street Journal reported earlier this week that Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent are bringing a new strategy to America's debt management.

Part of their plan is to extend the approach used by Janet Yellen, a plan both Bessent and Trump heavily criticized (and rightfully so), as the former Treasury Secretary relied on short-term debt to cover the Biden Administration's funding gap and avoid causing more volatility among long-term bonds.

Now, they are using short-term debt, hoping the replacement of Fed Chairman Jerome Powell will lower rates and allow them to refinance more sustainably.

Also loath to lock in borrowing costs that he thinks are too high, President Trump has suggested that Treasury should only issue debt that matures in about six to nine months until Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is replaced next year.

“What I’m going to do is I’m going to go very short-term,” he said in June. “Wait until this guy gets out, get the rates way down and then go long-term.” - WSJ

Using short-term bonds to fund the government can work out if the Fed were to cut rates. After all, it controls the short end of the curve. It does not control the yields on longer-term bonds. That's all controlled by Mr. Market.

Also, using short-term funding means the debt matures earlier. This increases volatility and makes the government much more prone to higher inflation.

If inflation were to accelerate, which I expect to happen, the Fed may even have to raise rates, making refinancing debt much more expensive.

As short-term government debt has ballooned, that's a huge problem.

This means a number of things, including the need to own a lot of inflation protection through equities, hard assets, or a mix of the two.

Unfortunately, that's not easy, as we cannot just buy random stocks.

Why?

Because the stock market is also unusual.

As I have discussed in a number of articles this year, the market's valuation has become an issue. Using the latest data below, we see the market has one of the highest forward P/E ratios ever, which implies two things:

  • We can assume future returns are subdued.
  • There is a lot of pressure on earnings growth, as lower-than-expected earnings could cause this ratio to rise even further, as forward P/E ratios include earnings expectations.

On a side note, this also explains why the market was so nervous in March and April. The 20% stock market sell-off could have been a lot worse if earnings had come down, as stocks were only cheap because earnings remained strong.

Another problem is that stocks are expensive while bonds are cheap. By that, I mean that the market's earnings yield (the inverse P/E ratio) is now equal to the yield on longer-term bonds.

Essentially, it tells investors there's little reason to take equity risk.

Hence, in order to keep stocks attractive, a number of things will have to occur. I think the bullet points below are the most important:

  • Exceptional future earnings growth: We need clear, sustainable evidence of strong corporate profit growth in the future.
  • Low/deflationary environment: When inflation is low, stocks offer better real return potential than bonds.
  • Aggressive shareholder returns: Significant stock buybacks and consistent dividend growth are needed to keep equities attractive.
  • Positive market sentiment: Broad investor optimism and a "risk-on" appetite. Any reason to avoid stocks can punish stocks badly.

So, Now What?

Given the high market valuations and the inflationary pressures tied to the government's short-term debt strategy, as well as general inflationary developments, equity investments should focus on sectors that thrive in cyclical upswings and offer inflation resilience.

I would focus on companies with strong fundamentals in industrials, energy, and materials, which are sectors poised to benefit from real economic growth, secular growth like infrastructure spending, and come with inflation protection.

Think of companies like Caterpillar (CAT) or Exxon Mobil (XOM), which can capitalize on rising demand and commodity price tailwinds.

It also includes other infrastructure plays like Brookfield Infrastructure Corp. (BIPC) or its partnership equivalent (BIP). BIP yields 4.1%, has grown its distribution/dividend by 9% per year since 2009, has low cyclical risks, 85% inflation-indexed or better pricing power, and exposure to some of the world's best infrastructure assets, as we can see below.

Even better, these companies often have solid balance sheets and consistent cash flows, which provide a buffer against volatility while they also align with my expected nominal GDP growth push.

Additionally, avoid overpaying for equities. Stick to names with reasonable P/E ratios and proven earnings stability to mitigate the risk of a market correction.

That's where midstream comes in, as they combine energy with infrastructure. It's an industry I have brought up a lot, as I think it's one of the best places to be. For example, companies like ONEOK (OKE) own thousands of miles of "mission-critical" pipelines, connecting major production areas like the Permian to export facilities and high-demand areas of natural gas liquids and crude oil.

OKE yields 5.0% and is expected to maintain 3-4% annual dividend growth, protected by close to zero direct exposure to commodity prices and elevated growth from new infrastructure projects and smart M&A.

If you don't mind non-American stocks, you'll like PrairieSky Royalty (PSK:CA) and Topaz Energy (TPZ:CA), which own mineral rights in Canada's best oil reserves. This allows them to benefit from both rising production and potentially higher prices without having to spend any capital on production because actual oil and gas producers take on these costs and pay royalties for the right to drill.

They yield 4.3% and 5.3%, respectively, and have done very well in recent years, benefiting from their income potential, increasingly favorable Canadian energy fundamentals, and the fact that they are cheap in a market that is not.

Moreover, I also like REITs for income, inflation protection, and growth.

However, in this segment, I am super picky (even pickier than usual), as many slow-growing REITs are nothing more than bond proxies.

That's why I like Rexford Industrial (REXR) and Prologis (PLD), which are two industrial REITs with a large footprint in high-demand areas like Southern California. In that area, Rexford has 100% of its assets, while Prologis has roughly a quarter of its assets in the area.

While Rexford has a bigger footprint in the local economy, Prologis is a warehouse-focused REIT. It owns some of the world's most advanced distribution/logistics assets and caters to top-tier tenants like Amazon (AMZN), which is its biggest tenant, providing secular tailwinds from e-commerce.

These REITs have decent income (REXRT has a 4.7% yield, while PLD has a 3.8% yield), and inflation protection from rent escalators and rising market rents, which opens up mark-to-market pricing opportunities, something I like way more than the boring and often low 2-3% contractual rent escalators.

Needless to say, I will spend a lot more time on this subject, as I think it's one of the most important topics that applies to governments, investors, and everyone prone to inflation.

The next few years could (read: likely will) be challenging, but if we make the right choices, we cannot only protect ourselves but even capitalize on it.

And, to be honest, given the assets we discuss so frequently on Seeking Alpha, I feel extremely good about what's next.

Takeaway

These are uncertain times, but they're also full of opportunity.

You just need to know where to look. That's the tricky part.

With regard to the Big Picture, I believe it's fair to say the government's aggressive growth strategy, fueled by short-term debt and a tolerance for higher inflation, sets the stage for a volatile market.

Luckily, this market also comes with opportunities, which we need to protect (and grow) our wealth.

With stocks expensive and bonds offering little incentive to take on equity risk, the key is to focus on sectors built for this environment, including industrials, energy, materials, and select REITs.

Companies like Caterpillar, ExxonMobil, and Brookfield Infrastructure offer inflation protection, strong cash flows, and exposure to real economic growth.

Meanwhile, midstream energy (like ONEOK) and Canadian royalty plays (like Topaz Energy and PrairieSky) add yield and stability in what has become my single-favorite sector for value and income.

All things considered, avoid overpaying for hype.

Stick to fundamentally strong, reasonably valued names that can weather inflation and benefit from what I expect to become a bottom in cyclical growth.

The road ahead won't be smooth, but with the right portfolio, we can both survive and thrive.

And, don't forget, life's good!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: July Jobs Report: Don't Bet On A Rate Cut
data.description:
The standout feature of the jobs report for July was ugly downward revisions to non-farm payroll figures for May and June. They were irredeemable even with a decent turnout in July.

Following the development, a rate cut is widely expected in September now. Here I argue that the overall macroeconomic picture doesn't entirely support the case for a rate cut, though. At the same time, the partial possibility of a worsening in the economy in the ensuing months calls for cautious investments in equities.

Non-farm payrolls' shock downward revision

The change in non-farm payrolls was a shocker with a massive 87% and 90% downward revision to data for May 2025 and June 2025 respectively. The absolute figures for the two months now stand at exceptionally muted levels of 19k and 14k. For context, the YTD average change is 85.3k.

By contrast, at least the initial July reading of 73k is much better, but it just isn't enough. It's still 14.4% smaller than the YTD average and 43% smaller than the 12-month average of 128k.

Unemployment rate just about inches up

The unemployment rate on the other hand inched up just a bit to 4.2% in July after retreating to 4.1% in June. The number is also slightly higher than the 12-month and YTD average of 4.14%, but is still at the modal value for the past 12 months (see chart below).

Essentially the unemployment rate signals that labor market conditions remain firm. This is especially so as the figure is still 0.3 percentage points [pp] lower than the Fed's projected rate of 4.5% for 2025.

An economy in flux

In a nutshell, the labor market release posts a mixed picture of the real economy. It only adds to the idea that the US economy is in flux, as indicated by the latest GDP release. With a 3% annualised rate of growth in Q2 2025, the economy looks healthy on the face of it, a pleasant surprise after a contraction in Q1 2025 (see chart below).

But this is due to lower imports, after some clarity on tariffs became visible since Q1 2025. The big consumer economy is actually flailing. Contribution to growth from consumer spendings was at just 0.98pp in Q2 2025 compared with 1.9pp in Q2 2024. More broadly, this contribution is down by 39.6% compared with the past year average.

Whether this softening in consumer spending will continue, however, remains to be seen considering that retail sales have grown at an average rate of 4.1% YoY in Q2 2025, up from 2.5% YoY in Q2 2024. If the consumer economy does remain strong, it could also feed into inflation, which is already inching up.

Inflation based on PCE , the Fed's target price index, rose to 2.6% YoY, the biggest in 4 months (see chart below). Core PCE sustained at 2.8% YoY, same as in May. Note that both numbers are below the Fed's forecast rate of 3% and 3.1% for headline and core figures respectively.

What's next for monetary policy?

The macroeconomy then suggests that there are arguments both in favour and against a rate cut when the Fed meets in September. The smaller than previously observed non-farm payroll numbers, lower contribution from consumer spending and PCE inflation still lower than the Fed's forecast make a case for a rate cut.

However, a sticky unemployment rate, also below the Fed's projections, bounce back in economic growth in Q2 2025 and healthier retail sales in the quarter YoY suggest that the Fed might not be inclined to slash rates too soon.

The markets are convinced, however, that a rate cut is coming in September following the labor market numbers. CME's Fedwatch based on the 30-Day Fed Funds futures prices shows that the probability of a 25bps rate cut has jumped from 37.7% on July 31, 2025 to over 80% as of August 1, 2025.

Where to invest now

During earnings season, it's hard to tell what's truly driving the stock markets. But if the labor market release drove them, the markets certainly weren't happy. The NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX), (NASDAQ:QQQ) showed the biggest drop of almost 2%, followed by the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, NYSEARCA:SPY), which fell by over 1.6% and then the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) by 1.3%.

Investors are right to be cautious, though. The big jump in probability of a rate cut could well reflect that the economy is expected to soften going forward, which in turn could hold back inflation. We will have to see.

In the meantime, avoiding cyclicals continues to be a good idea. Sure, there are some cyclical stocks that can be bought selectively, as I pointed out in my article following the FOMC meeting. But that can't be generalised.

Defensives continue to look good, which I won't detail here since they were mentioned in the same article from a couple of days ago. But the recent stagnation in gold prices is something worth highlighting.

The World Gold Council expects a 0-5% upside to gold prices in H2 2025. If the macroeconomy deteriorates, there could be more upside. Keeping in mind that the economy is in a flux right now, it wouldn't hurt to buy some of this safe haven investment even if there's limited upside for now. Buying stocks of high performing gold miners might be a good idea as well.

In Conclusion

The labor market report paints a mixed picture of the economy, which fits right in with the rest of the macroeconomic story. This implies that it can't be said for sure that the Fed will reduce rates in September, even if that's what the markets believe right now.

Note that probabilities for rate changes can alter depending on data developments moving forward. Much like they did post the latest jobs report. However, even this volatility reflects continued, if not amplified, uncertainty around the economy. In this environment, investors could do well to remain cautious. Besides defensives, investing in gold can stabilise the portfolio for now.

data.title: SOYB: A Gateway To Volatile Soybean Price Exposure
data.description:
Soybeans are oilseeds that are critical ingredients in food and fuel. Soybean products are soybean meal, which is used for animal feed, while soybean oil is required for cooking, as an ingredient in various foods, and for biofuel. Soybeans are crucial for biodiesel production.

While Brazil is the leading soybean-producing country, the United States is a close second, producing more than double as third-place Argentina.

Soybean, soybean meal, and soybean oil futures trade on the CBOT division of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange. While many commodity ETFs have soybean exposure, the Teucrium Soybean Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:SOYB) is the only product with dedicated soybean exposure to date at the time of this writing in August 2025. The SOYB ETF began trading in September 2011 with an AUM today of just under $25 million.

SOYB’s Overview

The Teucrium website highlights that “The Teucrium Soybean Fund provides investors an easy way to gain exposure to the price of soybean futures in a brokerage account.”

Seeking Alpha’s Fund Profile for SOYB states:

SOYB’s Objectives

SOYB’s primary objective is to mirror the price action in a portfolio of actively traded soybean futures contracts.

The Teucrium website highlights SOYB’s objectives:

Soybeans are one of the most liquidly traded agricultural products in the commodities market. Teucrium highlights that “SOYB may be held as a core component of your overall exposure to agriculture.”

Volatility of the Soybean ETF versus the Soybean futures market since inception

The highest volatility in a futures market typically occurs in the nearby contract, as it attracts the most speculative interest.

The monthly continuous contract chart of the CBOT soybean futures highlights monthly historical volatility at 11.52% in late July 2025. The range since June 2010 has been from 11.52% in July 2025 to 34.40% in November 2014.

The SOYB ETF exhibits a lower historical volatility of 9.23% in late July 2025. Moreover, the range since 2010 has also been lower than that of the continuous CBOT futures contract, ranging from 8.62% in April 2023 to 26.20% in December 2012 in the ETFs’ early days.

The bottom line is that SOYB’s portfolio of three active contracts results in lower historical volatility compared to the nearby soybean futures contract.

SOYB’s Strategy

Commodity prices can be highly volatile, and soybeans are not an exception. The term structure, or the price differential between delivery months, can be as volatile as the underlying price of the nearby contract. Moreover, agricultural commodities, such as soybeans, are susceptible to weather events and seasonality during the annual planting season in early spring, the growing season in spring and summer, and the harvest season in fall. Therefore, the soybean futures can experience significant price variance when the active month contract rolls to the next actively traded contract. Speculators and hedgers will either roll risk positions or liquidate during roll periods, adding to soybean’s price volatility.

Teucrium has addressed the “roll risk” by excluding the nearby futures contract. Owning three actively traded deferred futures contracts mitigates risks by eliminating the “roll risk” when the nearby contract nears its first delivery date.

The three-contract strategy, excluding the nearby futures contract, accounts for the SOYB ETF’s lower historical volatility compared to that of the continuous CBOT futures contract.

SOYB’s Holdings

Soybean futures trade on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange’s CBOT division. SOYB’s most recent top holdings include:

As the chart shows, SOYB invests its assets in three close to equally weighted soybean futures contracts.

SOYB offers options

The SOYB ETF offers put and call option contracts

The chart highlights the put and call offering for the SOYB ETF product. As of late July 2025, the options offered are for expiration on August 15, 2025, September 19, 2025, November 21, 2025, and February 20, 2026.

Risks with the SOYB ETF

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades highlight the risks associated with the SOYB ETF.

Soybean futures and the SOYB ETF have been in a bear market since the 2022 high that took soybean prices to the highest level since 2012, earning the ETF a failing F in momentum.

SOYB charges a 0.83% management fee. The high expense ratio earns a failing F grade for expenses. SOYB also pays no dividend.

The risk in the volatile soybean futures market, along with the bearish trend, receives a D grade in the risk category today.

Meanwhile, at $21.27 per share in early August, SOYB had $24.38 million in assets under management. SOYB trades an average of below 23,200 shares daily. Moreover, the low open interest, which represents the total of open long and short positions in the SOYB put and call options, receives a failing F grade in liquidity.

Illiquidity in the SOYB ETF is a leading risk. Meanwhile, the volatile nature of soybean futures over the past few years contributes to the ETF’s elevated risk.

Tax Considerations

The SOYB ETF is structured as a PTP, or a publicly traded partnership. Therefore, investors owning SOYB will receive an annual Schedule K-1 form, detailing the investor’s share of the fund’s profits, losses, and income.

K-1 reports tend to arrive later than most 1099s, which could delay tax filing.

SOYB’s futures contracts are subject to “mark-to-market” accounting. Therefore, any unrealized losses or gains in open positions are treated as if the contracts were sold, creating potential tax liability.

The 60/40 rule applies to gains and losses from commodity futures contracts. The split tax treatment means the IRS considers 60% of the gains to be long-term capital gains, taxed at the lower long-term capital gain rate. However, the IRS considers 40% of short-term capital gains to be taxed at the investor’s ordinary tax rate, which could be as high as 37%.

Investors can use losses from SOYB to offset capital gains and potentially up to $3,000 of ordinary income annually.

The states can have different treatment for the K-1 gains and losses.

No Peers Dedicated to the Soybean Market

While some diversified commodity ETF products have exposure to soybeans, there are no dedicated soybean ETF products, reflecting the soybean’s price action in the futures market. SOYB is the only ETF that provides a direct exposure to the soybean price through its portfolio of actively traded deferred CBOT soybean futures contracts.

Suitability

SOYB is suitable for investors and traders seeking exposure to the specific agricultural product. SOYB is an unleveraged product, so it does not experience time decay.

SOYB is not suitable for market participants unfamiliar with the risks associated with volatile commodity markets.

The bottom line is that ETFs like SOYB contain significant and complex tax considerations. Investors should consult a qualified tax advisor for personalized guidance for considering the suitability of these products.

Pros and Cons

SOYB’s Pros:

  • Direct exposure to one of the world’s leading and most liquid agricultural commodities, soybeans.
  • Exposure to soybean prices in standard brokerage accounts, avoiding the margin and other requirements of the futures markets.
  • Lower historical volatility than the soybean futures because of the deferred contract construction.
  • Mitigating the “roll risk” by excluding the nearby futures contract, which attracts more speculative interest.
  • SOYB can outperform the highly speculative nearby contract on the downside, limiting losses during bearish trends.
  • Diversification of portfolios with commodities, an alternative asset class.
  • Sector and individual diversification from exposure to soybean prices.

SOYB's Cons:

  • Tax considerations with an annual K-1.
  • Low liquidity in SOYB options and the ETF at times.
  • High expense ratio over the 0.80% level.
  • Most volatility occurs in the nearby soybean contract that attracts the most speculative interest. SOYB can underperform the nearby contract during bull markets that impact the nearby futures contract.
  • Soybean futures trade for extended hours in the futures market, while the SOYB ETF is only available during stock market hours. The ETF can miss highs or lows in the futures when the stock market is closed.

Conclusion

The SOYB ETF is the only option for investors and traders seeking exposure to soybean prices without venturing into the futures arena. Understanding the risks and potential rewards of agricultural commodity markets, as well as the idiosyncratic nature of the soybean market, is crucial when considering a risk position using the SOYB ETF.

This article answers these three main questions about MVV:

  • How does SOYB provide exposure to soybean prices?
  • What benefits and risks are there to investing in SOYB?
  • Which important elements of this ETF should investors consider before investing in SOYB?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Banco Bradesco Deepens Its Recovery, But The Market Still Waits
data.description:
Since my last update, Banco Bradesco (BBD) not only maintained its operational improvement path, but in Q2 2025 it managed to deepen several of the advances that I already anticipated.

The bank again showed solidity in its main metrics: recurring net profit reached R$6.1B, with an ROAE that rose to 14.6% and a credit growth that remained above the sector average. The profitability, efficiency, and quality of the portfolio continue to recover, while the expansion of the digital channel and new technological developments consolidate its competitive profile.

Despite this progress, BBD’s stock continues to operate with depressed multiples, partly due to the persistent market caution in the face of challenges imposed by the Brazilian context and the pressure of new digital players. Today the valuation discount is even more pronounced than in previous quarters, although fundamentals show a clear improvement.

For all this, I consider that the best option remains "hold": the results of Q2 reinforce the value thesis, but the catalyst for revaluation still depends on clearer signals on the macro and competitive front.

Overview

Bradesco (BBD) is going through 2025 as a bank that has been able to convert itself amid the technological pressure and transformation of the Brazilian financial system. Unlike other major players, the entity has opted for a balanced approach: it maintains its physical capillarity and its historical territorial roots, but advances in the deployment of digital solutions and the improvement of the customer experience, integrating technology and AI in virtually all its services.

In Q2, the bank reinforced its strategy of combining prudent loan origination with greater operational efficiency and a clear focus on high value-added segments such as individuals and SMEs. The expansion of the portfolio, with particular vigour in personal loans, real estate, and collateralized loans, was accompanied by controlled default and robust coverage ratios, while the weight of insured loans continued to grow within the total portfolio.

The digital channel, led by the Next ecosystem and the development of new platforms for companies, was consolidated as a key engine in both customer acquisition and revenue generation and commissions. At the same time, the physical network continues to play a strategic role in areas where financial inclusion still depends on face-to-face contact and human counselling.

BBD’s current model is based on the strength of its balance sheet, the strength of its insurance franchise, and an active dividend distribution policy, factors that allow it to navigate the ups and downs of the Brazilian economy with resilience.

Q2 2025

During Q2 2025, BBD consolidated its improvement trend and showed significant progress on almost all operational and financial fronts. BBD presented a net profit of R$6.1B (+28.6% YoY) and ROAE was at 14.6%.

Revenues were R$34B (+15.1% YoY), with expansions in all lines. The net financial margin amounted to R$18.044B (+15.8% YoY), while net interest income from clients was R$17.756B (+16.4% YoY). The average spread on the portfolio was 8.8%, showing a better combination of products and efficiency in anchoring.

The expanded credit portfolio for the first time exceeded the real trillion (R$1.018T), showing an 11.7% YoY advance. Growth was particularly robust in the segments of individuals (+15.9% YoY, up to R$442.4B) and SMEs (+25.2% YoY), while large companies remained practically stable. There was also an expansion in personal loans (+17.5% YoY), mortgage credits (+17.6% YoY), and rural credits (+46.3% YoY).

Portfolio quality remained under control, with late payments over 90 days remaining at 4.1%. The provisioning ratio largely covers problematic exposure, with levels above 100% on the at-risk portfolio. In addition, the restructured portfolio decreased by 21% YoY and now represents only 4% of the total portfolio, a clear sign of improvement in credit quality.

Operating revenue from commissions and fees grew by 10.6% YoY, reaching R$10,307B. The card business was highlighted, which increased its revenue by 19.9% YoY and reached a record R$4,460b, while revenues from consortia rose 20.8% and asset management 3.7%. The insurance segment delivered a net profit of R$2.3 billion (+4.4% YoY) and an ROAE of 21.4%.

Total operating expenses amounted to R$15,89b (+9.9%YoY), explained in part by higher investments in digital transformation and the impact of new consolidations. However, personnel and administrative expenses, which are the core of the operating cost, only grew by 4.9% YoY, below inflation, showing a firm control on recurring spending.

On the equity side, BBD closed Q2 with a Tier 1 capital ratio of 13%, while dividend policy remained aggressive, bringing the annualized dividend yield to 7.8%, more than double the industry average.

Valuation, Growth, And Profitability: An Asymmetry Difficult To Ignore

The current situation of BBD reveals a rare paradox in the financial system of LatAm: a bank that exhibits growth and profitability far above its peers, but which continues to quote at discount prices, as if the market ignores the progress of its balance sheet.

BBD trades at a P/E FWD of 6.70x, almost 38% under the industry average (10.79x) and well below its 5-year average (8.05x). In addition, the PEG FWD is 0.38x, so the share price doesn’t reflect the real and projected growth of earnings, moreover, the PEG of BBD is more than 69% lower than that of the sector (1.26x) and almost 40% below its own historical avg (0.62x), which shows us that the market discount is a more negative scenario than what the balance sheets show.

The P/B FWD is 0.97x, below both sector (1.14x) and its own history (1.12x). Meanwhile, the FWD P/S is down to 1.25x, less than half of the industry average (2.79x) and more than 47% below its own average.

This disconnect is most clear when we see that BBD currently pays a dividend yield of 7.83%, more than double the industry (3.05%) and also its own 5-year avg (3.61%). This means that, in addition to the multiple appreciation potential, BBD rewards the shareholder with CF, which is very relevant in the current context of positive real rates and regional volatility.

On the growth side, the difference with the sector is even more marked. In the last 12 months, BBD’s revenues grew 23.6% YoY vs 7.2% YoY of the sector, with an EPS growth of 44.9% (vs. sector: 10.6%). Projections remain strong: the estimated growth of EPS FWD is 14.7%, 50% higher than the sectoral avg and almost three times its own 5-year avg (5.2%). The ROE TTM is 11.14%, just below its historical level but still above the sector (10.56%), and BBD’s net margin remains robust at 22.3%.

Conclusion

BBD again exceeded expectations with another strong quarter in growth and profitability. The bank continues to improve where it imports and pays a dividend that is difficult to match, but the market still does not recognize it and the stock remains cheap.

Therefore, I still believe that it is best to maintain the position: the results accompany and, meanwhile, it is possible to collect a good flow, hoping that the market will finally value all this progress.

data.title: ArcelorMittal: Upgrading My Price Target After 12 Months
data.description:
It's been about 1 year since I last invested in and also covered steel company ArcelorMittal (NYSE:MT). It was never my biggest steel or basic materials investment, preferring companies like Norsk Hydro (OTCQX:NHYDY) instead, but it was still quite excellent for what it represented. While I missed out on an early exit in early 2025, when the company for a short time "peaked", I am looking at the company after a more than 40% RoR, inclusive of dividends. This article will explain my reasoning behind this new look, and what turns out to be a raising of my price target.

If you follow my work, you will note that I have been reinvesting and selling off several of my "winners" over the past weeks.

This is somewhat contrary to being a long-term investor, but I've been moving away, year by year, from being a buy-and-hold (forever) investor. I believe the investment style that suits me, and my approach the best, is more of an analysis-enter-analysis-exit approach, with a typical 40-200% RoR target (and usually no more than that - the investments I make typically don't have it in them to give 1000% or above).

My objective with ArcelorMittal was to beat the market. This objective was achieved with a very good margin, as you can see below. The question now is whether further market-beating returns are possible after the latest results.

Because now comes the "fun" part of that cycle - considering whether to take home the "bacon" and deciding where to go next - and also, in this case, explaining my logic to you.

Let's get started.

ArcelorMittal - Recalibrating targets after 12 months time

As I am writing this article, it's the 1st of August. This is a very fitting time, since it's almost to the date 12 months ago that I wrote my last article on ArcelorMittal. Steel and basic materials are notoriously volatile a segment to invest in, and the last 12 months have been no exception to what I consider in this case to be a rule.

The company remains, with a 40+ year history, one of the premier integrated steel/mining businesses on the globe. Focusing on steelmaking plants in 15 countries, I believe at any cheap time, this company can become a "BUY". My view on the steel industry can also be seen in my quite recent article on German steel company thyssenkrupp (OTCPK:TKAMY).

However, this company is in a very different situation. So, despite "selling both", I believe they have very different profiles. ArcelorMittal's US-based mining and sourcing operations are limited (but growing, with full ownership now as of 2Q25 in Calvert). Instead, its activities are focused on areas such as South America, Europe/Eastern Europe, Central America, Africa, India, and Canada. This is both a positive and a negative. It means that the exposure to tariffs is there, but it also means that the lack of correlation to the US-based market makes it a diversification away from other steel companies, in the same way that thyssenkrupp is a play in part on Europe.

ArcelorMittal has over 120,000 people working for it as well. My reason for investing in the company is, without a doubt, in part this geographical diversification, but also full integration. In some ways, it's a much bigger version of Swedish steel company SSAB, yet it has not rushed headlong into sustainable steel production (which I view as a significant risk).

This article is something I have waited on as well, because I wanted to wait for the 2Q25 results - they came in yesterday. Much like thyssenkrupp, the picture presented is "good, but volatile". The company's margins are growing, but so are the geopolitical and cyclical challenges. MT is selling at an EBITDA of $125 per tonne, compared to an average of below $90/tonne.

This showcases a significant operational improvement, owing in no small part to its integrated business model. However, part of its share price resilience and growth has no doubt been due to the company's continuing buybacks, which have been ongoing for years. It has added over $18/share back to the book value, because MT has bought back a staggering 38% of shares outstanding during that time. As of the 2Q25 period, and as of July 31st, 2025, it means that the book value stands at $72/share compared to a share price of only roughly $32/share for the ADR MT.

MT is a much more "working" company than thyssenkrupp is. The company's fundamentals are BBB rated, and debt is extremely low, less than 20% long-term debt to capital.

The company's other advantages for the 2Q25 period include record shipments from some of its volatile geographies (Liberia, for instance), while like thyssenkrupp expects advantages from the EU's trade policy adjustments and anti-dumping duties. That being said, the trend for pricing and for the market overall is somewhat challenged. Why?

Because China's steel industry remains at a significant overcapacity, and the nation's export of steel continues. Most of the near-term market fundamentals are about tariffs and trade balances - and like I spoke of in my thyssenkrupp article, the future for the steel industry seems to move to a more regionalized production model, as opposed to globalized trends, which actually works in favor of a company such as ArcelorMittal.

North America is a growth segment, and the European segment, in my view, is bound to see significant growth and positive impact from elevated spending for defense and infrastructure. The company's EU-based operations are well-suited for this, and this means that in Europe, there are significant advantages to the company.

The time for investing in risky projects seems to be past. The focus for most companies in the field seems to be safe, high-quality growth or M&A projects with baked-in positives such as profitability and cash flow from a very early stage. The number of such projects is, of course, low. However, the company's legacy somewhat riskier projects continue to show profitability and "pay dividends", as it were.

Given the company's geographical set of exposures, the risk for this company is non-trivial. Its Brazilian operations are subject to what I can only consider to be risky trends given the current tariff situation, but the company is diversified enough to possibly "plan away" from this. The recent upgrade from a BBB- to a BBB also reflects the company's resilience, not only in the eyes of the market but in the eyes of the credit market as well.

I would say that ArcelorMittal is one of the world's most well-diversified and attractive global steel producers at this time, and while all of these companies come with their own sets of risks, the risks for this one are some we're able to actually see rather well.

Upsides for the company include its significant ongoing growth projects, which are set to make it a player to be reckoned with in the US, including its new Alabama asset, as well as a JV stake in Vallourec to increase the company's margins. The recovery I forecasted in my last article has in part happened, but more upside is possible from a mixed European steel market and demand rebound, more M&A's, and better efficiencies. At least, that is what I can see on the upside side. I say that 2Q25, despite all of the ongoing macro challenges, was a good quarter that justifies taking a careful look at my assumptions and price target, and updating them accordingly.

ArcelorMittal - Raising my PT

In my last article on ArcelorMittal, I gave the company a $40/share PT, based on a fair value for the company of roughly $60/share. I'm raising that as of this time. The company is expected to grow 15-16% per year at the very least for the next 3 years. Furthermore, the 1.48% yield doesn't scare me away - mining and basic materials do not tend to have overly large yields.

But the upside, even now, is non-trivial. The valuation trends are confirmed by the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings model, which puts the company at no less than a B+ or higher in every single part, despite, at the time of my last article, being more of a "HOLD".

Arguments for rotation or selling at this time are that the company is fully priced if you want to take very low or no risks at all, especially if compared to peers like Nucor (NUE) and Steel Dynamics (STLD).

I disagree with that assessment. I believe the combination of geographical diversification, of superb operating track record, and of the massive reversal and growth potential that the company has on a forecast basis, that this company is not only better valued at an average weighted P/E of less than 9.1x, it has significantly better upside. I therefore believe that it is not the time for rotation of ArcelorMittal - something I would consider if the company breached perhaps my PT of $45/share.

I will forecast MT at a P/E of 14x, the 5-year average, and based on that and current growth estimates, we have yet another triple-digit RoR potential.

Even if we discount the company for its inability to hit targets (they mostly miss or hit), you could discount it severely and still get a 15% annualized upside or above. And on what basis are we discounting these estimates? I believe the exposure to Europe, the JVs, and the growth projects will materialize positively. It would take a significant headwind to put a roadblock in the path of this company's trends, and any roadblock like that is very likely to affect competitors, like NUE and STLD, and others as well, if perhaps not to the same extent.

The simple fact is, on the basis of earnings forecast, ArcelorMittal remains significantly undervalued, and in fact cheaper than its competition, except for high-risk plays such as thyssenkrupp.

Next to peers such as Nucor, MT compares very favorably. Nucor trades at an average weighted P/E of 17.25x, which is close to double that of MT. Even if we forecast at a 14x P/E, similar level, this company/peer does not hit 15% annualized, and at the 5-year average is in fact below 10% annualized.

And Nucor also misses its estimates and targets most of the time. So on a peer basis, this and other companies are not, in my view, more attractive than ArcelorMittal. I, in fact, believe the upside of ArcelorMittal and its diversification to be far more compelling in an investment here.

Analyst targets would concur. The native ticker MT trades at €27.5/share, with an average 13-analyst price target of €35.8/share, an upside of 14% at this time. Out of 13 analysts, 8 are at "BUY" or "outperform" for the company, indicating a decent amount of conviction here.

Based on this, and the fact that this represents about 50% of the company's recorded book value, I would say that ArcelorMittal is a "BUY".

I would therefore adjust my fair value target to be at least $65/share, up from $60, to account for buybacks, and for the PT to move from $40 to $45/share, making the company a "BUY" with a 15% annualized upside, even to something like a 12-13x P/E here.

Risks to my positive, continued thesis on the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Tariff risks are by far the largest to consider. It's not an invalid argument to say that we need to wait for the full impacts to be able to be calculated. Secondary risks, due also to its EU exposure, are its lack of ESG/sustainability development. ArcelorMittal is still mostly legacy, unlike companies like SSAB, and the cost to shift this, if this becomes too costly, will be massive. This is as of yet not included in the company's plans.

Beyond that, we're mostly talking historical correlations, such as MT being a historical underperformer - I believe the current situation rises above this.

My updated PT is therefore $45/share for the ADR MT.

Thesis

  • My updated thesis for ArcelorMittal is currently a positive one for one of the leaders in global steel. I view the company as investable on the basis of its sector-leading low debt and coverage and decent upside, even after 2Q25.
  • The company's recent performance details not only operational improvements and good upside based on reversal, as well as new growth projects/JVs, but also higher upside due to a high book value thanks to share buybacks.
  • Based on this, I retain my "BUY" rating, and I'm planning to add to my current position in the business. I am further upgrading my PT to $45/share.
  • My PT for MT is upgraded to $45/share, with the company currently trading at less than $32/share as of August 2025.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $45/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a “Buy rating as of August 2025.

data.title: Radian Group: Buyback And Solid Results Make Shares Attractive
data.description:
Shares of Radian Group (RDN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 7% of their value. While the private mortgage insurer has delivered excellent financial results, this has been priced in, and focus has shifted to the potential for earnings to decline as losses eventually normalize. Indeed, Q2 results were strong, but they have done little to meaningfully alter sentiment. Last covered shares in May, rating Radian a “buy.” Shares reached my $37 target, though they have since given back all of those gains. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Radian.

In the company’s second quarter, Radian Group earned $1.01, which beat estimates by $0.03 as revenue was basically flat to last year at $318 million. Earnings were flat to last year. However, I would note that results were boosted by a $5 million tax benefit. Otherwise, the quarter was essentially in-line with analyst estimates. Unlike peers, MGIC Investment Corp. (MTG), which reported a large beat, RDN is taking a more cautious approach to its reserves, causing earnings to be a bit lower.

RDN now has $277 billion of insurance in force, up about $4 billion from a year ago. The company generated $234 million of earned premiums, consistent with prior quarters. While the company is growing its insurance pool, there has been some pricing compression. RDN had a 38.1bp portfolio yield, flat sequentially and down 0.4bps from last year. With the mortgage insurance industry being incredibly profitable over the past four years, new capital has entered the segment, pressuring pricing to a degree.

Importantly, RDN is maintaining discipline, and I prefer its approach of maintaining portfolio quality, rather than chasing growth, especially given the tenuous positioning of the housing market currently. In Q2, Raidan wrote $14.3 billion of new policies, up slightly from $13.9 billion last year. Only 4.8% of borrowers had a FICO score below 680, down slightly from 5.1% a year ago. 16.7% of loans have a loan-to-value above 95%. Its existing policies have 20% 95%+ LTVs at origination (with ongoing monthly payments, this share is now substantially lower vs. at origination). This ~3.5% improvement speaks to RDN tightening underwriting given stretched housing affordability and softening prices.

As I have discussed in the past, I like to separate Radian’s exposure into two buckets. I view policies written in 2021 and earlier as having nearly no risk. Radian provides insurance to homeowners who cannot afford a 20% down payment. It faces losses when a borrower defaults and when the home is worth less than the remaining mortgage balance. Strong employment has limited defaults, and then given how much home prices have risen, most borrowers have so much equity that they can sell their house for more than the mortgage balance.

Now with each policy that Radian writes, the backbook’s share of the total business shrinks. Still, 2021 and earlier policies account for 41% of the portfolio. While 2022 is not a no risk pool, there is also substantial equity underpinning many of these loans. Because such a substantial portion of the portfolio is written before 2022, I expect loss rates to remain very low. Plus, given elevated rates today, refinancing activity is low, allowing a strong ~84% persistency in the portfolio.

I also think it is important to emphasize that while the housing market is softening, pricing is still flattish nationally. As such, we are not likely to see a pronounced surge in negative equity homes. That said, we are seeing prices soften, particularly in the Sun Belt, where there has been more construction activity, creating supply gluts. I expect mortgage rates to broadly remain in the 6.5-7.25% area, which will keep affordability pressures. My base case is for a 0-3% decline in home prices over the next year, though there is likely to be a weaker performance in states like Florida.

By definition, recent policies are more susceptible to home price declines as they were purchased at current market levels. As such, we are seeing the company take a fairly cautious approach to reserves for losses. In Q2, Radian added $12 million to reserves. However, I would note that inside this $12 million, it set aside $48 million for new policies, which was offset by $36 million of favorable developments in prior years. It continues to have excess legacy reserves because of the low defaults for 2021 and earlier that I noted above. Indeed, Radian has been achieving a 98% cure rate on 2021-2022 defaults, validating my view that these policies are nearly no loss.

The overall portfolio continues to perform solidly. RDN-insured borrowers have a 2.27% default rate, down 6 bps sequentially but up from 2.04% last year. The company’s decision to add reserves, which now total $383 million, reflects more conservatism about future policies, after largely holding reserves flat last year. These reserves should help to insulate profitability in the event we do see a moderate downturn in housing, and if home prices actually continue to rise, the company is likely positioned to release reserves next year. Overall, I view RDN’s reserving policy as more indicative of management’s general conservatism than a warning sign of an impending problem.

Elsewhere, RDN has a $6.5 billion investment portfolio, down $100 million from last year, as it returns capital to investors. Because the portfolio is slightly smaller, it generated $73 million of net investment income (“NII”), down $1 million from last year. This is a high-quality portfolio. Radian only owns $20 million of below-investment-grade rated bonds, and the majority of the portfolio is AA-rated or higher. Much of the portfolio is in mortgages and asset-backed securities that gradually amortize. I view fixed-rate reinvestment as likely to only be a modest tailwind to results.

Radian has a strong capital position, including $2 billion of excess available assets, though this was down $100 million sequentially due to capital returns. RDN also has a 19.2% debt to capital ratio, which I view as healthy. It has sent $400 million of dividends from the operating company to the holding company so far this year. It remains on track to send $795 million this year.

These intercompany flows are critical because it is the holding company that pays the common dividend and repurchases stock. As a result, RDN has $784 million of holding company liquidity, leaving it positioned for ongoing capital returns. It pays a 3.1% dividend yield, and in Q2, it did $223 million of repurchases. As a result, its share count is down over 10% from last year. I expect at least $350 million of H2 buybacks and more likely $400+ million.

Radian is well positioned to continue sending dividends to the holding company. As you can see below, with each year, about $450-500 million of capital is released. Now, as it writes new policies, it will need to set aside capital. Still, given the pace of roll-off, RDN should be able to dividend up at least 100% of net income in the coming years, which can support buybacks and a growing dividend.

Excluding its $273 million AOCI loss, the book value is $35.20. Given RDN’s conservative approach to reserves, I continue to believe shares should trade at least at book value. Back in May, I saw the company earning $3.85-$4.05. With Q2 results in hand, I now see the company earning $3.90-$4.05. As its backbook continues to shrink, I see run-rate EPS of closer to $3.70. After this quarter, I continue to see $37 as Radian’s fair value or 10x run-rate, and I believe the recent pullback has created an attractive opportunity. I am a buyer.

data.title: Barrick Mining: Stock Performance To Benefit From Sound Strategies
data.description:
Barrick Mining (NYSE:B) has multiple positive factors in its favor that could drive the stock to outperform over the next year or two. The stock has a reasonable valuation, leaving room to the upside as the company continues to grow. Barrick's above-average double-digit expected revenue and earnings growth should be the positive driver for the stock. Barrick has multiple strategies to drive strong long-term growth.

Barrick Mining's Company Background

Barrick operates as a Canada-based $37 billion market cap gold and copper miner. The company's strategy is to plan for long-term global exploration programs, designed to deliver a steady stream of new business opportunities. The company is focused primarily on growing organically through exploration instead of through acquisitions. Barrick Mining changed its name from Barrick Gold earlier this year.

Barrick Mining has 89 million ounces of proven and probable mineral reserves as of the end of 2024. The company grew this significantly from 77 million ounces as of the end of 2023. This was accomplished by the conversion of the Reko Diq copper-gold resources to mineral reserves in Pakistan. This conversion added 13 million ounces of gold and 7.3 million tonnes of copper. Barrick owns 50% of Reko Diq, which is one of the largest undeveloped copper-gold projects in the world. Reko Diq is currently under construction. The first production from Reko Diq is expected to be at the end of 2028.

In North America, the company operates the Nevada Gold mines: Carlin, Cortex, and Turquoise Ridge. Those three mines produced 342,000 ounces of gold in Q1 2025. Also in North America is the Fourmile mine which has a 2025 program of 16 drilling rigs, drilling 73 holes totaling 55km. This action at Fourmile is to get the mine to pre-feasibility.

In Canada, Barrick is doing generative work in the Southern Superior region to characterize fertility of different greenstone belts. The company is also has active exploration efforts underway in the Northern Cordillera and the Appalachia regions of Canada. Barrick sees 22 advanced opportunities with over 40 early-staged projects reviewed in Canada.

The company operates the Pueblo Viejo mine in the Dominican Republic. Pueblo Viejo has a plant expansion and mine life extension plan designed to increase throughput to 14 metric tons and to sustain gold production of over 800,000 ounces per year. This mine produced 74,000 ounces of gold in Q1 2025.

Barrick's Kabili gold mine in the Congo had lower gold production of 63k ounces of gold in Q1 2025 vs. 76k ounces in Q1 2024 due to planned lower grades processed through the plant. However, operational improvements and continued investments are expected to lead to better results in the 2nd half of 2025.

In Tanzania, Barrick operates the North Mara mine which produced 67k ounces of gold in Q1 2025 and the Bulyanhulu mine which produced 37k ounces of gold in Q1 2025. The company is making operational improvements in both at both of these mines.

The Lumwana mine in Zambia produced 27k ounces of copper in Q1 2025. This was up from the 22k ounces produced in Q1 2024, but down significantly from the record of 46k ounces produced in Q4 2024. Production decreased from Q4 due to lower grades. However, higher grades are expected to be fed beginning in Q2.

Growth Catalysts

Barrick has multiple catalysts to drive long-term growth. The company has sound strategies to drive this growth. Barrick's focus on Tier One gold assets and growing the copper portfolio can help provide the company with long-term profitable growth. Tier One gold assets have the potential to deliver a minimum of a 10-year life of production of over 500,000 ounces per year and are in the lower half of the industry cost curve. Tier One copper assets have a reserve potential of more than 5 million tonnes and are in the lower half of the industry cost curve. So, this strategy can help drive Barrick Mining's production while keeping costs contained.

Another strategy includes Barrick's effort to maintain a disciplined reserve and resource replacement. This involves long-term planning for ongoing organic growth. This is a more cost-effective strategy for long-term growth as an alternative to growing through acquisitions which are typically much more expensive.

Barrick also employs strict discipline to find quality growth assets that meet the company's conservative investment criteria. This strategy involves not taking on new debt and not diluting shareholders. Barrick's strategy is to increase its per share value through cash investments. These investments are sustained by Barrick's existing businesses.

Barrick has a strategy that has industry-leading global exploration programs. This enables the company to find new growth opportunities in areas throughout the world containing high-quality metals.

The company strives to maintain a strong balance sheet. Barrick has $4.1 billion in total cash and $4.7 billion in total debt leaving the company with net debt of $623 million. That's not a bad amount of net debt for a capital intensive business. Barrick has 2.9x more current assets than current liabilities and 3.3x more total assets than total liabilities for total equity of $33.6 billion. Barrick's largest assets are its $28.7 billion in property, plant, & equipment. The largest liabilities after long-term debt are $3.8 billion in deferred tax liability and $3.2 billion in other non-current liabilities.

Barrick stated in the Q1 earnings conference call that the company would pay a special dividend when net debt reaches zero to a positive $500 million in cash. That is something that investors should keep in mind since Barrick is only $600 million short of cash to hit zero net debt.

Barrick's strong cash position and cash flow gives the company the flexibility of growing the business organically which is a part of their strategy. The company had a trailing 12 month [TTM] operating cash flow of $4.9 billion. Over the TTM period, Barrick paid $693 million in dividends, repurchased $641 million of stock, and spent $3.2 billion in CapEx, leaving the company with unlevered free cash flow of $1.4 billion.

The strength in the price of gold is a positive catalyst for Barrick. Gold has remained above $3,000 per ounce over the past few months. According to JPMorgan (JPM), the price of gold is expected to continue higher with an average price of $3,675/oz. by Q4 2025. The strong price of gold ensures that Barrick will get a strong revenue boost from each ounce that is sold.

Valuation

Barrick Mining currently has a net asset value of $19.5 (total assets of 48.142 billion - total liabilities of $14.578 billion)/total outstanding shares of 1719.5. Barrick is trading slightly higher with a current stock price of $21.12. The stock looks reasonably valued from a NAV perspective.

To get another perspective, Barrick is trading at 10.5x expected EPS of $2.02 for 2025. This is below the sector median of 15.4. Barrick's forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 5.16 as compared to the sector median of 8.47. From this perspective, the stock looks undervalued.

I would put more weight on the P/NAV since that is the main valuation metric for miners. Investors might consider buying the stock whenever the price drops below the NAV. This can ensure that you are getting the stock at a great value.

Technical Perspective

Barrick's weekly chart above shows the volatile nature of the stock over a multiple-year period. The RSI (purple line in the middle of the chart) is still in bullish territory as it remains above the 50 level. The MACD (bottom of the chart) is also bullish as the blue MACD line remains above the red signal line and the histogram bars are in the positive green zone.

It is important to point out the volatile nature of the stock. The chart does show the large fluctuations in the price of over 20% within just a few months in both directions. Investors can take advantage of these swings by buying the dips.

It is possible that the stock dips again from here which it tends to do when the RSI moves above 60. The best buying opportunities were when the RSI dropped to about 40 or below. The buying opportunities can be crossed-checked with the Price/NAV valuation. Buy when the stock price falls below the NAV (when the Price/NAV drops below zero).

The next likely catalyst for the stock is the next earnings report scheduled for August 11, 2025 for Q2. Analysts are expecting Q2 revenue of $3.7 billion and normalized EPS of $0.46. I would expect the report to be positive given the strong price of gold which should help to boost revenue. Plus, revenue and earnings estimates have been revised upwards over the past 6 months.

Risks for the Stock

Barrick's stock tends to be highly correlated to the price of gold. Sharp drops in the price of gold can lead to significant declines for the stock. Lower gold prices mean that Barrick will earn less revenue from each ounce that it sells. The price of gold has been strong so far in 2025. However, there is no guarantee that the positive trend will continue. The company and stock can also be negatively impacted by declines in the price of copper.

The company is at risk of government actions in the regions where it operates. Governments could impose higher taxes or other regulations which could make doing business more expensive in those countries. Barrick needs to maintain positive partnerships among the countries that it operates in to prevent unfavorable actions for the company.

Barrick is at risk of catastrophic natural disasters in the areas where it operates. Natural disasters could lead to significant delays and unexpected costs associated with repairing/replacing the loss of equipment and returning to normal operations.

Barrick Mining: Long-Term Outlook

Barrick appears to have a sound multi-pronged strategy for long-term growth. The company's focus on Tier One gold assets and growing the copper portfolio should help drive strong growth, while keeping costs contained. Barrick's focus on resource replacement and organic growth is another key strategy that gives the company a competitive edge for long-term growth.

I like how the company effectively manages its balance sheet to strive for zero net debt. Barrick's asset-heavy balance sheet should provide protection from downturns as the company has the flexibility to sell assets if needed.

With a reasonable valuation from a NAV perspective, the stock has a chance to outperform the broader market due to Barrick's above average expected growth. Barrick is expected to grow revenue at 18% in 2025 and follow this up with another 18% growth in 2026 according to analysts' estimates. This would significantly exceed analysts' expectations for the S&P 500 (SPY) revenue growth of 5.3% for 2025 and 6.3% for 2026.

Barrick's above average expected earnings growth of 60% for 2025 and 25% for 2026 significantly exceeds analysts' expectations for the S&P 500 of 9.6% earnings growth for 2025 and 13.9% for 2026. Barrick's strategies have the likely potential to drive performance to meet or exceeds its estimates.
